LandformTopography.geo_descr: Alluvial Deposition
Alluvial Deposition
Vertisols of lithomorphic origin, due to the texture and high initial smectite content of the parent materials.
Vertisols of lithomorphic origin, due to the texture and high initial smectite content of the parent materials.
Alluvial Deposition
Clay minerals in the parent material and topographic position have favored smectite accumulation and the formation of Vertisols

It occupies the Guadalquivir River marshes with Quaternary gray clay deposits. These deposits extend into narrowing valleys of Tertiary and Triassic hills. The relief shows slight elevation differences, with higher edges due to adjacent clay deposition.
Vertisols of lithomorphic origin, due to the texture and high initial smectite content of the parent materials.

Triassic materials, primarily variegated marls and clays, vividly colored sandstones (red, yellow, green), gypsum, and limestone, without a clear spatial distribution pattern. The geomorphology is undulating, with variable slopes depending on the area.
Clay minerals in the parent material and topographic position have favored smectite accumulation and the formation of Vertisols
Vertisols of lithomorphic origin, due to the texture and high initial smectite content of the parent materials.

It occupies the Guadalquivir River marshes with Quaternary gray clay deposits. These deposits extend into narrowing valleys of Tertiary and Triassic hills. The relief shows slight elevation differences, with higher edges due to adjacent clay deposition.
The geological information indicates predominantly blue-gray marls and clays from the Upper Miocene-Pliocene. However, sandier materials are more abundant, leading to the absence of Vertisols.
Alluvial Deposition
This geomorphological unit is in a depressed area surrounded by gentle hills. The terrain is flat or nearly flat, with slopes generally not exceeding 1%.
This geomorphological unit is in a depressed area surrounded by gentle hills. The terrain is flat or nearly flat, with slopes generally not exceeding 1%.

The soils develop on Miocene white and gray marls with lower expandable clay and higher carbonate content, resulting in common calcic horizons. Lower areas show darker epipedons, sometimes forming mollic epipedons.

Its soils are largely settled on albarizas, a name commonly used to designate the relatively low-density siliceous marls of the Miocene. The terrain ranges from undulating to hilly, with sometimes steep slopes of up to 20%.

It occupies a significant overall area, being distributed across several zones of the Sheet. It appears in hilly areas, in the "countryside," with slopes that can reach up to 20%.

Located southeast of Lebrija, this unit is intersected by the roads connecting Lebrija to the N-IV, including the roads of Santa Luisa, Los Tollos, and El Cuervo. It is centered around the stream of Las Arenas in the depression called "La Herradura.

Inverted relief, where the ancient alluvial deposits (terraces) occupy the high positions of the landscape. The terrace platforms are located at heights between 130 and 145 meters, without it being possible to distinguish different levels or stages of the terrace.

The lands consist of recent alluvial deposits from rivers, mainly the Guadalquivir and Guadiamar and their key tributaries. These flat lands vary in nature based on the materials of the narrower tributary stream areas. They are primarily used for irrigated agriculture, including vegetable gardens and fruit orchards

These flat lands vary in nature based on the materials of the narrower tributary stream areas. They are primarily used for irrigated agriculture, including vegetable gardens and fruit orchards

The unit includes sloped lands linking the Aljarafe plateau with the Guadalquivir and Guadiamar plains and the Campo region. It extends from Palomares del Río to Castilleja de la Cuesta and Salteras, and from Olivares to Aznalcázar in Aljarafe Alto, and from Huévar to Castilleja and Escacena del Campo in Aljarafe Bajo. The region consists of Upper Miocene calcareous sandstones and yellow silty-clayey or sandy-calcareous silts. These soils are highly erosion-prone, especially on steeper, sparsely vegetated slopes.
Hill

The Campo region, situated between the Sierra Morena scarp and the Aljarafe peneplain, features gently undulating and flat terrains mainly composed of Miocene blue-green marls. The area is divided by the terraces of the Guadiamar River and calcareous lands of Albaida and Olivares. The eastern part has marl outcrops with some sandy areas, while the western part has more depressed, darker clayey soils with a drainage network flowing towards the Guadiamar River.

The Aljarafe region's most representative area is mainly red, flat, or gently undulating terrain, drained by rivers and streams. It includes small calcareous elevations intermixed with red soil. The region is divided into Aljarafe Alto, which lies between the Guadalquivir and Guadiamar rivers, and Aljarafe Bajo, west of the Guadiamar River.

The Aljarafe region's most representative area is mainly red, flat, or gently undulating terrain, drained by rivers and streams. It includes small calcareous elevations intermixed with red soil. The region is divided into Aljarafe Alto, which lies between the Guadalquivir and Guadiamar rivers, and Aljarafe Bajo, west of the Guadiamar River.

Ancient river terrace. In the study area, only the terraces of the Guadiamar River are represented. On the right bank, there are high terraces with sandy-pebbly soils used for holm oak pastures and cattle farming, and low terraces between the river's alluvium and Aljarafe's hydromorphic soils, used for annual crops and orchards.

Upper terrace. In the study area, only the terraces of the Guadiamar River are represented. On the right bank, there are high terraces with sandy-pebbly soils used for holm oak pastures and cattle farming, and low terraces between the river's alluvium and Aljarafe's hydromorphic soils, used for annual crops and orchards.

The soils, formed on Miocene Vindobonian marls or marly limestones, have moderately developed profiles and are calcareous throughout. They are found on gently undulating hills, mainly in the lower parts of gentle slopes.
The landforms are located in hilly areas, typically on the higher parts of hills or steep slopes, and are adjacent to vertisols found in lower, flatter regions or areas with very gentle slopes.

The soils, formed on Miocene Vindobonian marls, have slightly to moderately developed profiles, are calcareous, and range from shallow to moderately deep. They are found on undulating hills or on gentle to moderately steep slopes.

These soils are found in alluvial valleys, formed on recent fluvial deposits. They have deep profiles, a uniform content of calcium carbonate, no significant clay accumulation in the subsoil, and a slightly developed structure, typically forming flat or gently sloping terrains
